skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guides AI coding assistants through evidence-first debugging, root cause tracing, hypothesis testing, and verification before applying fixes.
skill.summary: Use a structured root-cause process before changing code.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: The static risk score is driven mostly by false positives in Markdown diagrams, TypeScript examples, and timeout strings; I found no evidence of weak cryptography, Ruby backtick execution, network exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts. Confirmed risk is medium because the package includes an executable shell helper that runs local tests and examples that may expose local environment or keychain details if copied directly. The skill appears intended for legitimate debugging and can be published with clear warnings.

medium_findings.title: Executable Helper Runs Local Test Commands
medium_findings.description: find-polluter.sh is an executable debugging helper that enumerates matching test files and runs npm test for each match. This is legitimate for finding test pollution, but it executes project code and should only be used in trusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell script directly runs npm test on discovered files, so the execution behavior is confirmed. The arguments are quoted and the purpose is debugging, so I do not see command injection or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Local Diagnostic Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes examples for listing macOS keychains, finding signing identities, printing selected environment state, and logging process context. These are useful diagnostics, but copied output may disclose local identity, environment, or path information.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly inspect local keychain, environment, cwd, and stack context. They are documentation examples rather than automatic exfiltration, which keeps the finding at medium risk.

low_findings.title: Static Findings Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Many detections come from Markdown diagrams, code fences, timeout messages, and TypeScript polling examples. I did not find evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms or Ruby backtick execution in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible as DOT diagrams, TypeScript examples, and timeout error strings. The semantic context does not contain cryptographic operations or shell backticks.

low_findings.title: Embedded Pressure-Test Prompts Need Context
low_findings.description: The pressure-test files contain direct scenario instructions to choose an answer. They appear to be validation prompts for the skill, not an attempt to override marketplace review or system instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The language is imperative and directed at an AI assistant, but the files are clearly labeled pressure tests. I found no explicit request to ignore instructions, skip security analysis, or change risk levels.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The package includes a shell script and documentation examples that run local commands. This pattern is expected for debugging, but users should review commands before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain concrete shell commands or a script command invocation. I found legitimate debugging context and no remote exfiltration behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local State Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples inspect filesystem paths, environment values, cwd, stack traces, or signing identities. This can reveal sensitive local context in logs or shared debugging transcripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The local inspection behavior is explicit in examples. The risk depends on how a user applies the examples, so this is a warning rather than a block.

content.user_title: Debug Bugs with Root Cause Discipline
content.value_statement: Random fixes waste time and create regressions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through evidence-first debugging before changes.
actual_capabilities: Guides a four-phase debugging process before proposing fixes.
Prompts reproduction, evidence gathering, and recent-change review.
Explains backward tracing from symptoms to original triggers.
Shows condition-based waiting patterns for flaky async tests.
Recommends validation layers after a root cause is found.
Includes a helper script for finding tests that create unwanted state.
limitations: It does not diagnose bugs automatically without project evidence.
The shell helper runs local tests and should be reviewed first.
Examples may need adaptation for each framework or repository.
The process can slow teams that only want a temporary workaround.

use_cases.title: Investigate Failing Tests
use_cases.description: Use the process to reproduce failures, identify timing issues, and replace guesses with condition-based checks.

use_cases.title: Stabilize Production Incidents
use_cases.description: Use structured evidence gathering before deciding whether a fix, rollback, or mitigation addresses the real cause.

use_cases.title: Review Debugging Plans
use_cases.description: Use the phases to challenge symptom fixes and require a clear hypothesis before code changes.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Bug Investigation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a new failure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging for this bug. First list the exact error, reproduction steps, recent changes, and missing evidence before suggesting any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Flaky Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for async or timing failures.
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply systematic-debugging to this flaky test. Identify every arbitrary wait, the condition it is trying to observe, and a safer condition-based replacement.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a Deep Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use root-cause tracing from systematic-debugging. Start at the symptom, trace each caller and data value backward, then state the first invalid input source.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Proposed Fix
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt before implementing a change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed fix with systematic-debugging. Decide whether root cause evidence exists, name the hypothesis, and list the smallest verification step.

output_examples.input: A payment test sometimes receives pending instead of completed.
output_examples.output: The response should identify the timing assumption, ask what event confirms completion, and propose waiting for that event instead of adding longer sleeps.

output_examples.input: A git command created files in the source directory during tests.
output_examples.output: The response should trace the working directory value backward through callers and find where an empty or invalid path first entered the flow.

output_examples.input: A teammate suggests a quick retry to fix a production timeout.
output_examples.output: The response should separate emergency mitigation from root cause analysis and require evidence before treating the retry as the final fix.

best_practices: Collect reproduction steps and error details before proposing changes.
Test one hypothesis at a time with the smallest useful change.
After fixing the source, add validation at layers that can receive bad data.
anti_patterns: Adding sleeps, retries, or guards without evidence of the root cause.
Bundling several changes and then guessing which change fixed the issue.
Treating a senior opinion or past incident as proof without checking the current system.

faq.question: Does this skill fix bugs for me?
faq.answer: No. It structures the investigation so the assistant can find and verify the root cause before changing code.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it for test failures, production bugs, build failures, performance issues, and unexpected behavior.

faq.question: Can it help with flaky tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for replacing arbitrary waits with checks for the condition the test actually needs.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a shell helper that runs local tests to find state pollution. Review it before use.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Some examples log paths, environment context, stack traces, or signing identity information.

faq.question: Can I skip the process during incidents?
faq.answer: You can mitigate emergencies, but the skill treats mitigation and final root-cause fixes as separate decisions.
